title: Reading your compost heap temperature
subtitle: What the numbers mean and when to intervene
excerpt: A compost thermometer in the centre of an active hot heap tells you whether the heap is working, has peaked, needs turning, or has stalled. The four temperature zones each call for a different response.
sourceNotes: HDRA organic gardening technical notes on hot composting. Garden Organic guidance on compost temperature interpretation.

glossaryTerms.definition: An actively managed compost heap that reaches internal temperatures of 55-70 degrees C through microbial activity. Hot composting kills weed seeds and pathogens and produces finished compost in 8-12 weeks.

The ratio of carbon-rich (brown) material to nitrogen-rich (green) material in a compost heap. The optimal range for hot composting is 25:1 to 30:1 by weight; too much carbon slows decomposition, too much nitrogen causes anaerobic conditions and odour.

content.text: A compost thermometer is a 300-600 mm stainless-steel probe with a dial or digital display. It reads the internal temperature of the heap where the thermometer is inserted. In an active
hot compost
heap, the centre temperature is the most important single number in managing the process. Push the probe at least 300 mm into the centre of the heap, not near the edge.
The four temperature zones
Below 35 degrees C: heap not active
The microbial community is not generating enough heat to drive hot composting. Causes: heap too small (below 0.8 m3 it loses heat as fast as it is generated),
C:N ratio
too high (too many browns, too little nitrogen), heap too dry, or ambient temperature below 5 degrees C.
Response: add nitrogen-rich greens (fresh grass clippings, vegetable scraps, coffee grounds), check moisture and water if dry, then turn the heap to add air.
35-45 degrees C: mesophilic zone

Mesophilic bacteria are active and generating heat. The heap is composting but has not yet reached the temperature required to kill weed seeds (above 55 degrees C). This temperature range is typical of a heap building toward full activity, a heap cooling after a peak, or a heap with a modest nitrogen supply.
Response: leave it alone if it is on the way up. If it has been in this zone for more than a week without rising, add greens and turn.
45-70 degrees C: thermophilic zone

Thermophilic bacteria are active. At 55 degrees C maintained for three days, most weed seeds are killed. At 65 degrees C for three days, most pathogens are destroyed. This is the working temperature of a correctly managed hot heap. It may last two to five days in a single flush before the nitrogen supply in the outer layers is exhausted and the temperature drops.

Response: when the temperature begins to fall from the peak (typically from 65-70 degrees C to 50-55 degrees C), turn the heap to move the cooler outer layers into the centre. This recharges the microbial activity and extends the thermophilic phase.
Above 70 degrees C: overheating

Above 70 degrees C, thermophilic bacteria start to die. The microbial community shifts to heat-resistant spore-forming species that break down material more slowly. This happens most in heaps with a lot of fresh manure or concentrated nitrogen. Turn the heap at once to remove excess heat and rebalance the nitrogen.
The typical temperature curve

A well-managed hot heap shows a temperature curve that looks like this: rises from ambient to 50-65 degrees C within two to three days of filling. Holds in the thermophilic zone for three to five days. Drops back to 40-50 degrees C as the centre cools. First turning brings it back to 55-65 degrees C within 24 hours. Second turning (after a further 10-14 days) shows a slower rise to 45-55 degrees C. Third turning shows the heap stabilising at 35-40 degrees C, indicating that the easily decomposable material is exhausted and maturation is beginning.
When the thermometer is not the answer

A cold heap left alone for 9-12 months will produce finished compost without reaching thermophilic temperatures. Temperature monitoring is for hot composting where speed matters. For a cold static heap, the thermometer mainly confirms the heap is at ambient temperature and not hot composting, which is fine for a low-effort approach.
